session.title: Where a VLM Fine-Tune Commits Depends on the Task
session.abstract: Nanonets-OCR2-3B is the largest public-weight checkpoint in a family whose flagship (Nanonets-OCR-3, 35B MoE) tops the IDP Leaderboard. Because OCR2-3B shares a public base --- Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct --- it is a clean object for a question the flagship cannot answer: where does an OCR fine-tune actually commit? We apply four complementary diagnostics to 26 document images: residual-stream L2 diffs, head-level attention-pattern diffs, residual-stream activation patching, and per-module weight diffs. The residual signal concentrates in the upper decoder and peaks at L35 with Pearson $r = 0.996$ between two independently-trained fine-tunes of the same base. Per-image activation patching gives a sharper result than our pilot on 4 docvqa images implied: the causal-threshold layer is bimodal. Eleven of 26 images flip at layers 0--2 --- all four arXiv-equation pages, handwriting, and non-Latin scripts. Fifteen of 26 flip at layers 9--15 --- English typewritten documents, forms, receipts, and one Chinese page. Median threshold $L = 10$. Under a corrected reference (Nanonets-OCR2-3B's shipped config mis-sets top-level tie\_word\_embeddings; we use the intended tied projection), the full res
session.keyFinding: Full residual-stream patching is causally OCR-directional (92.3% peak match under a corrected reference, 26/26 images), head-level attention patching is not (0/26), and the causal-commitment layer is bimodal by content type — input layers for content the base reads poorly, mid-decoder for content it reads well.

paper.title: Where a VLM Fine-Tune Commits Depends on the Task: Differential and Causal Localization in Nanonets-OCR2-3B
paper.abstract: Nanonets-OCR2-3B is the largest public-weight checkpoint in a family whose flagship (Nanonets-OCR-3, 35B MoE) tops the IDP Leaderboard. Because OCR2-3B shares a public base --- Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct --- it is a clean object for a question the flagship cannot answer: where does an OCR fine-tune actually commit? We apply four complementary diagnostics to 26 document images: residual-stream L2 diffs, head-level attention-pattern diffs, residual-stream activation patching, and per-module weight diffs. The residual signal concentrates in the upper decoder and peaks at L35 with Pearson $r = 0.996$ between two independently-trained fine-tunes of the same base. Per-image activation patching gives a sharper result than our pilot on 4 docvqa images implied: the causal-threshold layer is bimodal. Eleven of 26 images flip at layers 0--2 --- all four arXiv-equation pages, handwriting, and non-Latin scripts. Fifteen of 26 flip at layers 9--15 --- English typewritten documents, forms, receipts, and one Chinese page. Median threshold $L = 10$. Under a corrected reference (Nanonets-OCR2-3B's shipped config mis-sets top-level tie\_word\_embeddings; we use the intended tied projection), the full residual-stream patch is genuinely OCR-directional: 26 of 26 images eventually produce OCR2's corrected top-1 at some layer, with a peak match rate of 92.3\% at L34 and a median first-match layer of 16. A random-matched-magnitude residual control produces 3.85\% peak match (1/26 images, 2 of that image's 36 layer-trials), a 24$$ differential that rules out a generic perturbation-tolerance reading. The fine-tune's causal commitment enters at input layers for content the base Qwen2.5-VL reads poorly, and at mid-decoder for content the base already reads. The router head L11.H14 from hua2025conflicting ranks 141 of 576 in our attention-pattern diff. A head-level causal test shows that no single-head L11 patch and no full-L11 attention patch redirects base toward OCR2's top-1: patched predictions never match OCR2. What the head-level test does show is a destabilization gradient --- H14 destabilizes 3/26 images, a low-Frobenius L11 control (H7) destabilizes 2/26, the highest-Frobenius L11 head (H8) destabilizes 1/26, and the full-layer swap destabilizes 1/26. The three H14-destabilized images are 2/4 arXiv and 1/4 handwritten. The mid-decoder residual-level threshold must therefore involve L11 MLP and cross-layer dynamics, not L11 attention alone. Within the top-12 Frobenius-diff heads, the L0 cluster loses structured-marker attention (input re-encoding) and the L30--33 cluster gains it (output specialization). MLPs carry 69\% of the weight change; rank-16 SVD of the per-module diff recovers 39--67\% depending on the module, so a rank-16 LoRA on mlp.gate\_proj and mlp.up\_proj captures the largest share under a compact rank budget but is not a full-fidelity reproduction. The L35 residual peak is text-dominated (53\% pre-image instruction tokens, 27\% post-image, 20\% image toke

guide.title: Understanding Guide: Differential Localization in a VLM Fine-Tune
guide.subtitle: A companion to the paper — field context, key concepts, the decision log behind the research, and methodology notes for reproducing on a laptop.
guide.mdxContent:
# Field Landscape — Where this paper sits *Companion guide to "At Layer 11, the Fine-Tune Wins: Differential and Causal Localization of an OCR-Specialized VLM" (Ali, 2026). This section locates the paper inside the broader interpretability lineage. Audience: a Master's-level reader who knows what a transformer is but hasn't spent a weekend with `nnsight`.* ## Language-model interpretability, briefly The background to all of this is roughly eight years of trying to figure out what transformers do internally. Early circuit work on small language models (the Anthropic threads from 2021–2023, the ROME paper by Meng et al. at NeurIPS 2022) produced a workable vocabulary: *residual streams*, *attention heads*, *MLP factual associations*, *causal tracing*, *logit lens*. The working story for decoder-only LLMs is now roughly: - Early layers handle tokenization-adjacent stuff — syntax, position, basic lexical bindings. - Middle layers build factual associations in the MLP weights (Meng and co-authors localized factual recall to mid-stack FFNs, and then successfully edited facts by modifying those weights). - Later layers refine, select, and shape output distributions. There are clean interventional results that sit on top of this picture. The refusal-direction paper by Arditi et al. (NeurIPS 2024) is a good example: fine-tuning a model for safety adds a one-dimensional direction in residual space, and zeroing that direction turns refusal off. One number, one behavior, one geometric story. That's the shape of a satisfying mechanistic result, and it's the genre the paper this guide is attached to is trying to participate in. ## Multimodal interpretability is younger and messier Vision-language models (VLMs) add two wrinkles. First, the information flow is bimodal — visual patches come in at a specific span, get projected into the LM's embedding space, and then both image and text tokens share the same residual stream from that point on. Second, tasks often require the model to *selectively* use one modality depending on the instruction. ("What's written on the sign?" — read the image. "Is this grammatical?" — ignore the image.) Work up through 2024 mostly studied VLMs in isolation — one trained model at a time, perturbing inputs and watching what moved. The most useful paper in this lineage is Golovanevsky et al.'s NOTICE (arXiv:2406.16320), which corrupts either the image or the text and tracks which heads respond. On BLIP and LLaVA they identified "universal cross-attention heads" that function consistently across modalities. In 2025 this got sharper. Hua et al. (arXiv:2507.01790) ran the same style of experiment on Qwen2.5-VL specifically under *conflicting* inputs — the image says dog, the caption says cat — and recovered a clean three-way head taxonomy for the 7B variant: - **L11.H14** — a *router* head. Modality-agnostic; tracks whichever modality the instruction asks for. - **L19.H26** — an *image promotion* head. Always favors vision. - **L13.H26** — a *caption promotion* head. Always favors text. This is the cleanest existing coordinate system for the Qwen2.5-VL family's cross-modal wiring, and it's the one our paper leans on. A subtle architectural note that matters for what we do: Hua indexed their heads against the 7B variant, which has 28 heads per layer. The 3B model we analyze has 16 heads per layer. So H26 doesn't even exist in our model's address space — we can only cross-check L11.H14. Two parallel threads are worth knowing about. Steinberg and Gal (arXiv:2602.22918) asked "where does OCR *live* inside a trained VLM?" and found the OCR signal is surprisingly low-dimensional (PC1 captures ~73% of variance), with the peak layer depending on architecture — mid-depth for DeepStack models, earlier for single-stage projection models. Baek et al. (arXiv:2505.15865) identified "OCR heads" in Qwen2-VL-7B and InternVL2-8B using needle-in-a-haystack tests and found that masking the top-20 OCR heads drops DocVQA by ~20%. Both of these papers take a single trained model and ask where OCR lives inside it. ## 2026 — causal methods catch up Through 2024–2025, most VLM interp work was *correlational*: measure what's different, not what's functionally responsible. Causal activation patching — taking an activation from one context and injecting it into another — was the standard tool in LLM circuit work (Meng et al. 2022, Heimersheim & Nanda 2024) but adoption in VLMs lagged. The three-pass template (clean run → corrupted run → patched run) started landing on VLMs in late 2025 and early 2026: - **Li et al. (FCCT, arXiv:2511.05923, AAAI 2025)** — three-pass causal tracing across MHSA, FFN, and residual states in VLMs. Introduces a Recovery Rate metric. Training-free, forward-pass only. - **Yang et al. (arXiv:2602.20330, Feb 2026)** — full circuit tracing in VLMs via transcoders and attribution graphs on Gemma-3-4B-it. - **Che et al. (arXiv:2603.18523)** — counting circuits in Qwen2.5-VL-7B via Visual Activation Patching. 43 of 784 heads (5.5%) are functionally critical for counting. Our paper uses the FCCT three-pass template but adapts it to the *differential* setting: instead of swapping clean/corrupted inputs for the same model, we swap activations between two models (OCR2 and its base) on the same input. ## The differential question nobody asked Here is the gap the paper this guide is attached to tries to fill. All of the work above studies single trained models. None of it asks: > *What did supervised fine-tuning move?* For LLMs there are two partial answers — Arditi on refusal directions, the LoRA-interpretability thread (Ma et al. arXiv:2509.08454 on Whisper; Wang et al. arXiv:2502.11812's CircuitLoRA). For VLMs, the differential question has essentially not been asked, and it has particularly not been asked for OCR-specialized VLMs. This is a useful gap because OCR fine-tunes have crisp behavioral signatures. Nanonets OCR2-3B emits structured markdown tokens (``, ``, ``, ``) that the base model does not. If something moved inside the model to make those tokens more likely, the diff should show us where. ## Why this angle is a natural next question Three existing results converge to make this a reasonable thing to ask: 1. **Clean base.** Nanonets-OCR2-3B's base (Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct) is public and has an existing head taxonomy (Hua et al.) to anchor against. 2. **Two fine-tunes from the same base.** Nanonets shipped two independent fine-tunes of Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct — OCR-s (June 2025) and OCR2-3B (later). If the diff to the base is similar across both, what we're seeing is a property of the base's geometry, not of any one training run's data. 3. **Causal tools are now available on consumer hardware.** The FCCT three-pass template is forward-pass-only. Our whole study runs on an M4 Pro with 24 GB of unified memory. It's no longer the case that causal VLM interpretability needs an 80 GB H100. What falls out of asking this question — a residual-stream peak at L35, a causal threshold at L11, 61% of the weight change in the MLP blocks, the Hua router head sitting at the 24.5th percentile of the attention-diff — is the main content of the paper. The field-landscape framing for that content is: **we are taking tools developed for single-model VLM interpretability (Hua's taxonomy, FCCT-style causal tracing, attention-pattern analysis) and applying them differentially, to a base vs. fine-tune pair, in the OCR domain specifically.** That's not a method contribution — we don't invent a new probe — but it's a question-shape contribution: asking *what moved*, not *what's there*. If the closest neighbors to think about are: - **Hua et al. 2025** — we borrow their head taxonomy, we check one specific head (L11.H14) against our diff. - **Steinberg & Gal 2026** — they ask where OCR lives in a trained model; we ask where fine-tuning moved the representations. Same broad topic, different measurement. Both can be true simultaneously. - **Wang et al. 2025 (CircuitLoRA)** — they do fine-tune-diff circuit analysis on LLMs; we do the equivalent on a VLM, and derive a LoRA prescription from the weight diff. - **Li et al. 2025 (FCCT)** — we adapt their three-pass causal-tracing framework to the differential setting. — then the paper sits roughly at the intersection of those four, answering a question that each of them is adjacent to but none of them have tackled. --- # Key Concepts — a primer for the paper *The paper uses a specific vocabulary — residual streams, attention matrices, activation patching, head taxonomies. If you've read one or two interp blog posts but never actually hooked into a model, this is the field kit. Each concept gets its definition, why it matters for the Nanonets-OCR2 diff, and what to look for in the paper.* ## Attention matrices — what `A[L, H]` actually is At every decoder layer `L` of a transformer there are `H` attention heads. Each head, for each input sequence, produces a matrix ``` A[L, H](x) ∈ R^{T_q × T_k} ``` where `T_q` and `T_k` are the number of query tokens and key tokens. In a decoder-only autoregressive model like Qwen2.5-VL-3B, the matrix is square (`T × T`) and lower-triangular (causal mask). Entry `A[L, H][i, j]` is the weight that token `i` gave to token `j` when computing its attention output. Each row sums to 1. For Qwen2.5-VL-3B: 36 decoder layers × 16 heads/layer = **576 heads total**. For a 300-token input that's 576 matrices of shape 300×300 per image. That's a lot of data. The analysis in the paper is about how to summarize it. Two numbers you can extract from a single head's matrix that show up in the paper: - **Entropy of the attention distribution** — a high-entropy head is spreading its attention across many key tokens (broad attention); low-entropy is focused (spike attention). - **Max weight** — the largest single value in the row. A max-weight near 1.0 means the head is nearly doing a one-hot pick of its target. The analysis pipeline in `04_attention_diff.py` computes these per head to label heads heuristically. These heuristic labels are *not* the same thing as Hua et al.'s router/promoter labels (see below) — the paper is careful about this distinction. **Why it matters for this paper.** Claim 2 in the paper is "which heads behave differently in OCR2 vs. base." The object we measure is the Frobenius-norm distance between two attention matrices for the same head on the same input: ``` δ[L, H](x) = ‖A_OCR2[L, H](x) − A_base[L, H](x)‖_F ``` Aggregated over 26 images, this gives a single number per head. Rank the 576 heads by this number, look at the top 12. That's Figure 2. **A trap the paper discusses.** A top-12 head by `δ` is one whose *attention pattern changed a lot* — it does not mean the head is functionally responsible for anything. Heads can shift attention without changing the model's output, and vice versa. The paper's vocabulary paragraph in §3 is explicit: we say these heads are "perturbed" or "shifted," never "caused the change." ## Residual streams — the reason we L2-diff activations, not weights The *residual stream* is the cumulative hidden state that flows through the transformer. At each layer `L`, attention and MLP blocks read from it and add their outputs back to it. So the state at layer `L` is: ``` h_L(x, t) = h_{L-1}(x, t) + attn_L(h_{L-1}) + mlp_L(attn_output) ``` for token position `t`. In Qwen2.5-VL-3B, each `h_L(x, t)` is a vector of dimension 2048. Two properties matter. **First, the residual stream is where work accumulates.** Early layers write a little; later layers write more; by the end of the stack the vector encodes everything the model has computed about the token in context. This is why L2-diff between two models at the same layer is interpretable: it measures *how much the accumulated work diverged* by that layer. **Second, the residual stream naturally grows in L2 norm with depth.** This is the caveat the paper addresses: a rising diff through the layers isn't automatically a fine-tuning signature — some of it is just norm growth. The paper argues (and the synthesis doc argues) that the 100→214 jump from L30 to L35 is too sharp to be baseline drift; the prior 30 layers contribute only a 4× rise. **Weight diff vs. activation diff — why the paper does both.** - **Activation (residual) diff** measures *effects* — where in the stack do the two models' internal representations diverge on this input? - **Weight diff** measures *cause* — which parameters in the model were changed by fine-tuning? These tell different stories. The weight-diff figure (Figure 5) shows that fractional weight change is roughly uniform across all 36 layers (0.06–0.09). But the activation-diff figure (Figure 1) shows that effects concentrate in L30–L35. The reason: small weight deltas *compound* through the forward pass. A 7% parameter change at L10 doesn't produce much residual divergence at L10 itself, but by the time the computation has threaded through 25 more layers, the divergence has ramped. This is a useful thing to internalize for interp work: weight-space and activation-space answer different questions, and often disagree about "where the fine-tune acted." ## Head taxonomy — router, promoter, neither Hua et al. (arXiv:2507.01790) classified VLM heads by how they respond to *conflicting* image/text inputs. Their protocol is a 21-point α-sweep: for each value of α in [0, 1], the head's output attention is set to α × image-attention + (1−α) × text-attention, and the model's output is measured. This produces a curve per head. Three curve shapes get three labels: - **Router head** — the curve tracks the instruction. If the instruction says "describe the image" the head puts its attention on image tokens; if it says "paraphrase the caption" it puts attention on text tokens. Modality-agnostic; follows the task. - **Image promotion head** — the curve always favors image tokens, regardless of instruction. - **Caption promotion head** — always favors text tokens. - **Neither** — curve is ambiguous or flat. For Qwen2.5-VL-7B Hua et al. identified exactly one router (L11.H14) and two promoters (L19.H26 image, L13.H26 caption). L11.H14 is the only head we can cross-check because the 3B variant we analyze has 16 heads per layer — H26 doesn't exist in our address space. **Why it matters.** The paper's §4.3 makes a specific prior-grounded claim: if OCR fine-tuning rewires cross-modal arbitration, the router head should show up in our top-12 attention-diff. It doesn't — L11.H14 ranks 141 of 576, at the 24.5th percentile. This is the single cleanest finding in the paper: cross-modal arbitration is stable; the fine-tune operates elsewhere. The paper is disciplined about not applying the router/promoter labels to *its own* heuristic overlays (entropy threshold, max-attention threshold). Those heuristics get different names in the paper ("high-Frobenius heads") because applying Hua's labels without running Hua's α-sweep would be misuse. ## Activation patching — the "inject and watch" causal test Activation patching is the causal test the paper uses for Claim 1. It answers the question: *if I force the base model to have OCR2's internal state at layer L, does the base model then produce OCR2's output?* The procedure (following the Li et al. FCCT three-pass template, adapted to the differential setting): 1. **Clean run** — feed image x through OCR2. Cache the residual-stream vector at layer L, call it `h^{OCR2}_L(x)`. 2. **Natural run** — feed the same image x through the base. Record its next-token distribution `π_base(x)`. 3. **Patched run** — feed x through the base *again*, but when execution reaches layer L+1, replace the residual stream with the cached `h^{OCR2}_L(x)` from step 1. Let the base's layers L+1 through 35 continue from there. Record the resulting distribution `π_patched(x, L)`. Two numbers come out of this: - **KL divergence** `KL(π_patched ‖ π_base)` — how far did the patched output shift from the base's natural output? - **Top-1 flip rate** — on what fraction of images does the patched base's top-1 predicted token equal OCR2's top-1 predicted token? Sweep this over all 36 layers. At the layer where the flip rate hits 100%, the base's downstream layers can no longer recover their own answer from OCR2's residual — the fine-tune has committed by that point. For our paper this happens at **L = 11**. That's the causal threshold. Before L11, the base's remaining layers can still pull the distribution back to their own answer on half the test images. At and after L11, they cannot. **What the method does and doesn't show.** Patching is a *sufficiency* test. It shows OCR2's state at L11 is sufficient for the base to produce OCR2's output. It doesn't show the fine-tune *needs* layer L11 specifically — that would require a necessity test (ablate L11 in OCR2, measure degradation), which is listed as future work. The paper is careful about this ("we call L=11 the causal threshold; we call L=35 the magnitude peak; they are different things"). Caveat the paper owns: four images is small. The specific L=11 location should re-run on all 26 before being treated as a model-wide constant. ## Differential interpretability — what it means to compare two models All of the above tools are typically applied to *one* model. The paper's contribution shape is: apply them to *two* models that share a base, and look at the difference. What you get from comparing two models sharing a base (OCR2 vs. Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct): - **Structural control.** Same architecture, same tokenizer, same number of heads and layers. The residuals and attention matrices have the same shape. You can element-wise subtract. - **Initialization control.** The fine-tune started from the base's weights, so every non-zero diff is *caused by fine-tuning* — you don't have to rule out different initialization. - **Triangulation via a third model.** The paper runs the same diff against Nanonets-OCR-s (an earlier fine-tune of the same base) and checks whether both fine-tunes agree on where the diff concentrates. They do — at Pearson r = 0.996 on the 37-layer residual-diff curve. Two independently-trained fine-tunes agreeing that closely is evidence that the concentration is a property of the base's geometry, not of any single training run's data. The technique has a strong history in LLM work (Arditi et al.'s refusal direction is effectively a one-dimensional differential; the LoRA-interp thread is a weight-space differential). For VLMs it's been done mostly once — our paper is one of the early applications. ## Weight-space vs. activation-space — why the paper runs both The three activation-diff methods (residual L2, attention-pattern Frobenius, residual patching) measure what fine-tuning does to the *state* of the model during a forward pass. The weight diff measures what fine-tuning does to the *parameters* themselves. The paper's per-module weight diff (Figure 5): ``` Δ_module, layer = ‖W^{OCR2}_{module, layer} − W^{base}_{module, layer}‖_F ``` summed per module slot (q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj, mlp.gate_proj, mlp.up_proj, mlp.down_proj) across the 36 decoder layers. Result: the three MLP projections account for 69% of total Frobenius change. Attention projections account for 31% — and within attention, queries and outputs move more than keys and values. This has an operational reading: for a team doing a Qwen2.5-VL-class OCR fine-tune, a rank-16 LoRA on `mlp.gate_proj` and `mlp.up_proj` across all 36 layers captures most of what Nanonets moved. (The mock reviewer pushes back on this — "rank-16 SVD recovery of the Frobenius norm" is not the same as "rank-16 LoRA trained from scratch would match OCR2's DocVQA." The paper owns the caveat in §5.) ## A visual summary If you want one picture in your head for the four methods: ``` WEIGHT-SPACE (Figure 5) | | what changed in the parameters? v ACTIVATION-DIFF CAUSAL-PATCH (Figures 1, 2) (Figure 4) | | | where do internal states diverge? | where is OCR2's state sufficient | | to flip the base's output? v v residual-stream L2 replace base's h_L with OCR2's, peaks at L35 (r=0.996 measure KL & top-1 flip rate. across two fine-tunes) 100% flip at L≥11. attention Frobenius bimodal: L0, L30–33 ``` Claims 1 and 2 come from the activation-diff side. The causal threshold comes from patching. The LoRA prescription comes from the weight diff. The four converge on the same picture: upper decoder, MLP-heavy, cross-modal arbitration left alone. ## Quick reference | Term | One-sentence definition | Where in paper | |---|---|---| | Residual stream | Cumulative hidden-state vector at each layer (size 2048 for Qwen2.5-VL-3B). | §4.1, Fig 1 | | Attention matrix `A[L,H](x)` | T×T softmax weight matrix for layer L head H on input x. | §4.4, Fig 2 | | Frobenius-diff δ[L,H] | Matrix-norm distance between OCR2's and base's attention matrices for the same head. | §4.4, Fig 2 | | Router head (Hua) | VLM head that tracks whichever modality the instruction asks for. | §4.3 | | Promoter head (Hua) | VLM head that always favors one modality. | §4.3 | | Activation patching | Inject one model's residual into another's forward pass; measure output shift. | §4.2, Fig 4 | | Top-1 flip rate | Fraction of images on which the patched model's argmax equals the donor model's argmax. | §4.2 | | KL divergence | How far the patched distribution shifted from the natural one. | §4.2 | | Weight-diff Frobenius | Parameter-space L2 distance between the fine-tune and base per module slot. | §4.5, Fig 5 | | Rank-r SVD recovery | Fraction of weight-diff energy captured by a rank-r approximation — a LoRA feasibility proxy. | §4.5 | That should be enough to read the Results section without stopping to look anything up. --- # Decision Log — what we picked, what we cut, what surprised us *This file records the research decisions that shaped the paper: what methods we chose, what we decided not to include, and where the data disagreed with our initial framing and we had to walk things back. Reading this log alongside the paper should make clear why the paper looks the way it does.* ## Arc in one paragraph The paper started as a correlational study — compare a fine-tuned OCR VLM (Nanonets-OCR2-3B) to its public base (Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct) and measure where the representations diverge most. Two methods to start: residual-stream L2 diff as the anchor, per-head attention-pattern Frobenius diff as the headline. We were going to stop there. Reading the literature during orient-phase surfaced FCCT-style residual-stream activation patching (Li et al. 2025), which turned out to be runnable on MPS at layer granularity for about 30 minutes of compute — so we added it as the causal test the correlational methods cannot do on their own. Then we added a weight-space per-module diff because the question "so which layers should I put LoRA on?" is natural once you have the residual-stream picture, and it answers it from parameter space rather than activation space. Four methods, not two, with a cleaner causal story than the original plan. ## The Q0–Q8 framing decisions ### Q0 — The base-vs-fine-tune differential framing The paper asks "where in the model did fine-tuning act," not "what's the best OCR VLM." Three alternatives were on the table: - **Benchmark-style comparison.** Run multiple OCR VLMs on a test set, report the winner. Rejected: that is the IDP Leaderboard's job, and running more models adds noise to what is already a tight epistemic question. - **Differential interpretability.** Base → fine-tune diff analysis. **Chosen.** - **From-scratch probe design.** Build a new interpretability method and apply it to one model. Rejected: method-building risk is high for a fixed-time research window; the field already has useful tools (Hua's head taxonomy, FCCT-style patching) that give us coordinates to work from. ### Q1 — Method stack **Decision before any experiments:** residual-stream L2 diff + per-head attention-pattern Frobenius diff. Structured-output-mode probe as a stretch goal. Activation patching and SAE training excluded for compute reasons. **What changed during the run:** residual-stream activation patching went from excluded → added as a central claim after the deep-search phase surfaced Li et al.'s FCCT three-pass framework (clean / corrupted / patched). The compute estimate turned out to be tractable on MPS — about 30 minutes per direction at layer granularity. Adding patching is the single biggest quality improvement to the paper. It took the central claim from correlational ("residual diff peaks at L35") to causal ("patching OCR2's residual into the base flips the top-1 at per-image threshold layers"). The title changed to reflect this addition. Weight-space per-module diff was added even later, after a second literature pass surfaced Wang et al.'s CircuitLoRA work as a useful bridge between interpretability and parameter-efficient fine-tuning. The residual diff tells you where *effects* concentrate; the weight diff tells you which *modules* actually changed. Running both answers a natural "so what does this imply for adaptation?" question that the residual-diff-alone paper could not answer. ### Q2 — Dataset: 26 curated images 6 categories × 4 images each (plus 2 extra DocVQA) = 26 images. Categories chosen for heterogeneity of content: | Category | Source | Count | |---|---|---| | Printed mixed-layout | DocVQA val split | 6 | | Handwritten | IAM lines | 4 | | Scientific with LaTeX | arXiv crops | 4 | | Receipts | SROIE | 4 | | Forms | FUNSD | 4 | | Multilingual (zh / ar / ja / fr) | Rendered text | 4 | **What we considered:** a single-category set (just DocVQA) would be statistically more comfortable, but the cross-category argument — every category peaks at the same layer — is only possible with category diversity. We took the statistical cost to get the robustness argument. The cost shows up in §4.2's bimodal threshold, which needs N ≥ 20 across multiple content categories to reveal the split — a single-category sample would have given a misleadingly unimodal result. ### Q3 — Compute: consumer hardware, MPS, sequential model loading All experiments ran on a laptop with 24 GB of unified memory. Two consequences: - **Sequential loading, never parallel.** Load one model, cache activations to disk, free, load the next. Total wall-clock for activation extraction across three models was about 30 minutes. - **bfloat16 everywhere.** Full float32 weight differencing between two 3B models at once OOMs a 24 GB system. The weight-diff script streams per-layer state dicts at bf16, diffs on CPU, releases. A mechanistic interpretability paper demonstrating real results on a laptop is, we think, a better artifact than one that requires an 80 GB GPU. This is a principled choice, not just a resource constraint. ### Q4 — Paper length Target: 8 pages for the ICML MI Workshop long format. The current draft exceeds this — the planned camera-ready will move the ViT-perturbation section and per-rank-layer detail to an appendix. ### Q5 — Which claims to commit to **Decision at spec time:** - **Claim 1 (anchor):** layer localization of residual-stream diff. - **Claim 2 (headline):** a small set of attention heads specialize in structured-output emission. - **Claim 3 (stretch):** linearly-decodable "structured-output mode" direction. - **Claim 4 (cut pre-run):** handwriting MLP routing — required additional probe design we didn't have time to derisk. **What happened to Claim 3.** The original probe — logistic regression to distinguish structured-marker token positions from content-token positions — turned out to be *position-trivial* at the implementation step. The instruction prompt places structured markers at a fixed suffix position, so any classifier trained on (activation, position) pairs predicts the label from position alone. We pivoted to image-token-vs-text-token modality-boundary probing. The pivot probe returned 100% / 100% on both base and OCR2 — a ceiling-effect null; modality decodability at layer 0 is linearly trivial, a known fact we rediscovered. Claim 3 was demoted to a methods-section footnote. **Claim that took over the title.** Activation patching was not in the original claim inventory. The causal-threshold result became prominent enough to replace the original framing. The paper's current title reflects the causal story, not the correlational one. ### Q6 — Vocabulary discipline Early drafts used "router" and "promoter" for the top-12 Frobenius-diff heads. A synthesis pass flagged this as conflating our heuristic labels with Hua et al.'s rigorous α-sweep classification. The final paper: - Uses the label "high-Frobenius heads" for our top-12. - Reserves "router" for one specific reference (L11.H14's rank in our diff). - Does not label any of our heads as "promoters." Tradeoff: we lose an easy compound narrative. We gain the ability to defend our vocabulary in front of a mechanistic-interpretability reviewer. Right tradeoff. ### Q7 — The "Nanonets-actionable" paragraph Early drafts had a discussion-section paragraph framed as "what a team scaling this methodology could do with the weight-diff signature." A mock-reviewer pass flagged the framing as borderline promotional. The camera-ready phrasing is "Implications for differential adaptation," and the content is generic: rank-16 LoRA on MLP projections + content-conditional LoRA placement + NAST-style layer-wise learning-rate analogue. The ICML MI Workshop is a scientific venue; the paper's implications should read as useful for anyone working on similar problems, not as a report targeted at a specific company. ### Q8 — Narrator companion All five narrator files are published with the paper: field landscape, key concepts, decision log (this file), methodology guide, comprehension questions. The intent is that a reader can use the narrator to build or refresh the mental model the paper assumes, rather than the paper itself having to spend pages on background. The decision log exists because the *why* of research decisions is harder to reconstruct after the fact than the *what*. ## Runtime decisions (things that moved during the experiments) ### `attn_implementation="eager"` — the setting that cost 10 minutes to rediscover HuggingFace transformers on Qwen2.5-VL defaults to `sdpa` (scaled dot-product attention), with `flash_attention_2` available as an option. Both of those are the fast paths. Both of them, when `output_attentions=True` is also set, return an empty tuple instead of per-head attention probabilities — because the fast kernels don't materialize the T×T softmax matrix. The paper's entire head-wise analysis depends on having that matrix. ```python model = AutoModelForImageTextToText.from_pretrained( repo, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device_map="mps", attn_implementation="eager", # ← this line ) ``` Without `eager`, Phase 2 would run to completion and save files full of zero-dimensional tensors. The first attempt did exactly that. Ten minutes of "why are the attention tensors empty?" debugging, then eager, then everything worked. Worth flagging for anyone else hook-walking a Qwen2-family VLM. ### Streaming weight diff The first attempt at the weight-diff script (`09_weight_diff_lora_candidates.py`) tried to hold both 3B models in CPU memory simultaneously for element-wise subtraction. OOM. Rewrote as `09b_weight_diff_streaming.py`: 1. Load base at bf16, device_map="cpu". Extract per-layer state dicts. Free the model. 2. Load OCR2 at bf16, device_map="cpu". Extract per-layer state dicts. Free the model. 3. Iterate layer-by-layer; at each layer, subtract the small per-module weight tensors. Cost: ~15 extra minutes; paid off in every subsequent rerun. The lesson generalizes — for interpretability on commodity hardware, assume you cannot hold two full model snapshots in memory at once and design accordingly. ### Single-direction patching first, bidirectional later The initial spec had both directions of residual patching (OCR2 → base and base → OCR2). We ran the forward direction first, which was sufficient to make the causal-threshold claim in §4.2. The reverse direction was added later as §4.8 and gave a cleanly surprising result: base residuals destabilize OCR2 but never redirect it toward base's prediction. The asymmetry between forward and reverse is one of the paper's more informative findings, and we would have missed it by staying single-direction. ### Dropping "router" and "promoter" for our heuristic head labels The Phase 4 self-review flagged that using Hua et al.'s vocabulary for our heuristically-identified high-Frobenius-diff heads would conflate a weak classifier with a rigorous one. See Q6 above. ## What surprised us ### Cross-pair Pearson r = 0.996 on the residual-diff curve The spec anticipated "similar layer-localization patterns across panels" as corroboration for Claim 1. We were expecting Pearson r in the 0.7–0.9 range — similar but not identical. The actual number is 0.996 on a 37-point curve. Two independently-trained fine-tunes of the same base, on different data, producing residual-diff curves that track each other at that correlation is unusually clean and makes the localization claim much harder to dismiss than a single-pair result would be. ### The causal threshold is bimodal, not unimodal A pilot on 4 docvqa-heavy images suggested a clean step function at L = 11 — every tested image flipped there, no image flipped earlier. We almost titled the paper around that. Running the same experiment on the full 26-image set overturned the sharpness: 11 of 26 images flip at layers 0–2 (arXiv equations, handwriting, non-Latin scripts), 15 of 26 flip at layers 9–15 (English typewritten, forms, receipts). The L = 11 reading was an artifact of a docvqa-heavy sample. The bimodal reading is richer: the fine-tune's causal commitment enters at different pipeline stages for different content types. Heterogeneous content, not just large N, was load-bearing for the finding. ### The L11.H14 router head's attention pattern is stable under fine-tuning Hua et al.'s taxonomy marks L11.H14 as the cross-modal router. We expected it to either (a) light up strongly in our attention-diff as the fine-tune rewired cross-modal arbitration, or (b) land somewhere middling. It landed at rank 141 of 576 — 24.5th percentile from the top, nowhere near the top-tier shifts. A later head-level patching test confirmed the attention-pattern reading is the right one for this head: patching H14 alone doesn't redirect base's prediction toward OCR2's. The fine-tune is not acting through the router head's attention pattern, or through any L11 attention component in isolation. ### MLPs carry 69% of the weight change; attention barely touches keys and values The per-module cumulative weight diff showed `mlp.gate_proj`, `mlp.up_proj`, and `mlp.down_proj` accounting for 69% of the total Frobenius change. Attention projections account for 31%, and within attention, query and output projections dominate; keys and values barely move. The fine-tune rewires how information is computed inside each layer's FFN, and how outputs are formed from attention reads, rather than what keys are compared against. The LoRA-placement recommendation in §5 is a direct consequence. ### The vision encoder moves in OCR2 but not in OCR-s Our initial framing treated the shared ViT as effectively unchanged across OCR2, OCR-s, and base. A direct weight diff on OCR2's ViT overturned the "unchanged" part (11.2% mean fractional change, 1.6× the decoder's per-layer average). Then the symmetric measurement on OCR-s gave 1.1% — an order of magnitude lower. Two fine-tunes of the same base can make very different choices about how much the vision encoder moves during training. Whether to unfreeze the ViT is a training-recipe decision, not a structural requirement of OCR fine-tuning. This is the single clearest example in the paper of a result walking back an earlier framing — and of why having two independent fine-tunes of the same base (rather than just one) was worth the cost of a second set of activations. ### Head-level patching never redirects the prediction, even when residual-level does The v4 draft of §4.7 reported that patching only OCR2's L11.H14 slice into base's forward flips 3 of 26 top-1 predictions, and interpreted this as evidence the head is mechanistically active. A mock-reviewer pass asked for a single-head control, which we ran. Under the control, H14 destabilizes 3/26, a low-Frobenius L11 control (H7) destabilizes 2/26, the highest-Frobenius L11 head (H8) destabilizes 1/26, and the full-layer swap destabilizes 1/26 — a gradient, yes, but *no* intervention ever produces base predictions that match OCR2's corrected top-1. The v4 "H14 is mechanistically active" reading was overreaching. The right reading is that full residual-stream patching redirects (92.3% match at peak) while head-level attention patching does not, and the mid-cluster causal threshold therefore lives in L11's MLP or in cross-layer residual dynamics. The single-head control was the experiment that sharpened the claim; without it, §4.7 would have been an over-interpretation. ## What we would do next Ranked by value-to-effort, items the paper does not include but the current infrastructure supports: 1. **Per-head patching sweep across all 576 heads.** The §4.7 L11 sweep was a targeted check; a full sweep would identify whether any head at any layer produces an OCR-directional flip, and would falsify or confirm the "full residual state, not any single head" reading definitively. 2. **Trained rank-16 LoRA on `mlp.gate_proj` + `mlp.up_proj`.** SVD recovery curves predict a substantial portion of MLP Frobenius energy is captured at rank 16 per module. Actually training the adapter and evaluating on DocVQA would turn the weight-diff observation into a functional claim. 3. **Larger per-category N.** The bimodal-threshold split is well-separated at N = 11 vs N = 15 per cluster, but within-cluster ranking (e.g., handwriting vs LaTeX) would require more per-category images. 4. **Content-conditional probe for structured-output mode.** The v1 ceiling-effect null was position-trivial; a probe on *content* rather than position could still work. Requires prompt-set design that avoids the trivial giveaway. 5. **ViT head-level attention-pattern diff for both fine-tunes.** The per-block weight diff in §4.6 is the coarse version; head-level attention on the ViT would localize where OCR2 and OCR-s disagreed during training. Items 1, 2, and 3 are all tractable continuations; items 4 and 5 are longer. ## Voice The paper is written in the voice of a mechanistic-interpretability blog post — specific, opinionated, willing to say "two honest caveats" and "the result surprised us" without qualifier stacking. We have not used the standard conference-paper hedges ("we conjecture," "seems to suggest"). Where findings are correlational, we say so. Where they are causal, we say so. Where they are both partial and useful, we say that. --- # Methodology Guide — reproducing the paper *Step-by-step setup on an M4 Pro (or any MPS-capable Apple Silicon). The pipeline is forward-pass-only — no training, no gradients, no cloud. If you have 24 GB of unified memory, a recent Python, and ~30 GB of free disk you can run the whole thing in roughly an hour of wall clock.* ## Environment From the repo root (`theoria-nanonets-ocr-interp/`): ```bash python3 -m venv .venv source .venv/bin/activate pip install -r code/requirements.txt hf auth login # Nanonets checkpoints sometimes rate-limit anonymous pulls ``` Key dependencies: `torch` (MPS backend), `transformers`, `nnsight` v0.6.3, `datasets`, `matplotlib`, `pdf2image`. The `requirements.txt` pins versions — use those versions rather than bleeding edge, because the Qwen2.5-VL handling has been churning. On a fresh machine, the first forward pass downloads 22.5 GB of model weights (three 3.75 B VLMs at bf16). You want to be on fast internet for that. After that, everything is local. ## Pipeline overview ``` 01_build_eval_set.py # 26 document images across 6 categories ↓ data/processed/eval_set_manifest.json 02_extract_activations.py # forward pass per model, cache hidden + attn tensors ↓ code/activations/{base,ocr2,ocr_s}/image_NN_cat.pt 03_residual_diff.py # L2 diff per layer → Figure 1 04_attention_diff.py # per-head Frobenius diff → Figure 2 08b_residual_patching_lite.py # residual-stream patching, base ← OCR2 → Figure 4 09b_weight_diff_streaming.py # per-module weight diff → Figure 5 ``` Stage 05 (the structured-output probe) is in the repo but the probe is trivial — documented as a methods paragraph in the paper rather than a figure. ## Stage 1 — Build the eval set ```bash python code/experiments/01_build_eval_set.py ``` What it does: assembles 26 document images across six categories — DocVQA (6), IAM handwriting (4), arXiv LaTeX crops (4), SROIE receipts (4), FUNSD forms (4), rendered multilingual text in zh/ar/ja/fr (4). Each image is resized so that its longer side is 896 px; the processor will downsample further to produce ≈256 image tokens per image. Two things to watch for: - **SROIE and FUNSD** have flaky HuggingFace mirrors. The build script falls back to matplotlib-rendered placeholder receipts/forms if the datasets time out. In the paper we used the real sources; if your run falls back to placeholders, the diff figures will still be computable but the per-category breakdowns will differ. - **Multilingual.** We use matplotlib-rendered Unicode text blocks in zh/ar/ja/fr rather than Wikipedia screenshots. This is a simplification documented in the spec §5 footnote. The rendered blocks are consistent and reproducible; they're also somewhat easier to read than real scans, which may explain why the per-category diff for multilingual is in the middle of the spread rather than at the extreme. Output: `data/processed/eval_set_manifest.json` with per-image SHA-256 hashes. If a later rerun on the same manifest produces different hashes, someone updated the upstream dataset. ## Stage 2 — Extract activations This is the slow stage. Three models × 26 images = 78 forward passes, each saving hidden states and attention tensors to disk. Allocate ~10 minutes per model on an M4 Pro. ```bash python code/experiments/02_extract_activations.py --model base python code/experiments/02_extract_activations.py --model ocr2 python code/experiments/02_extract_activations.py --model ocr_s ``` Three things in this script are load-bearing. **The eager attention trap.** HuggingFace transformers defaults Qwen2.5-VL to `attn_implementation="sdpa"`. With `output_attentions=True`, `sdpa` returns empty tuples — it doesn't materialize the T×T softmax. You have to override: ```python model = AutoModelForImageTextToText.from_pretrained( repo, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device_map="mps", attn_implementation="eager", ) ``` The same trap applies to `flash_attention_2`. Eager is the only implementation that populates per-head attention tensors. Cost: slightly slower forward pass. Benefit: you have the data the paper needs. **Sequential model loading.** 24 GB of unified memory will not hold three 3.75 B VLMs concurrently. The script loads one model, runs 26 forward passes, writes activations to disk, releases the model and its processor, empties the MPS cache, and only then loads the next: ```python del model, processor gc.collect() if torch.backends.mps.is_available(): torch.mps.empty_cache() ``` **Fixed image-token budget.** To make cross-model activation tensors compatible for element-wise subtraction, every image is processed to a consistent ~256 LLM image tokens: ```python TARGET_TOKENS = 256 PIXELS_PER_TOKEN = 28 * 28 MIN_PIXELS = TARGET_TOKENS * PIXELS_PER_TOKEN MAX_PIXELS = TARGET_TOKENS * PIXELS_PER_TOKEN ``` `min_pixels == max_pixels` pins the image token count exactly. Without this, the attention matrix shape varies per image and per model, and the per-head diff computation becomes a broadcast nightmare. **Per-image output.** Each call saves one `.pt` file containing hidden states (37 layers, each of shape `(T, 2048)`), attentions (36 layers, each of shape `(16, T, T)`), input ids, token strings, and the image-token span. bf16 on disk. Total: ~18 GB for all 78 files. ## Stage 3 — Residual-stream L2 diff ```bash python code/experiments/03_residual_diff.py ``` For each image, load the base and fine-tune payloads from Stage 2. Align token counts (they should match; trim if not). Compute per-layer, per-token L2 distance: ```python def compute_pair_diff(tag_a, tag_b, pairs): diffs = [] for image_id, category in pairs: h_a = load_hidden(tag_a, image_id, category) # list of (T, 2048), len 37 h_b = load_hidden(tag_b, image_id, category) per_layer = [] for l in range(len(h_a)): a, b = align_token_count(h_a[l], h_b[l]) per_token_l2 = (a - b).norm(dim=-1) # (T,) per_layer.append(per_token_l2.mean().item()) diffs.append(per_layer) return np.array(diffs) # (n_images, 37) ``` Run once for (OCR2, base) and once for (OCR-s, base). Compute Pearson correlation between the two 37-length image-averaged curves. That Pearson is the r=0.996 number the paper leans on. Output: `code/results/residual_diff.json` and `code/figures/fig1_residual_diff.pdf`. ## Stage 4 — Attention-pattern Frobenius diff ```bash python code/experiments/04_attention_diff.py ``` Load attention tensors for the (OCR2, base) pair, per image. For each of 576 heads, compute per-image Frobenius norm of the pattern difference: ```python # for layer L in [0..35], head H in [0..15], image x: A_ocr2 = payload_ocr2["attentions"][L][0, H] # (T, T) A_base = payload_base["attentions"][L][0, H] delta[L, H, x] = (A_ocr2.float() - A_base.float()).norm(p='fro').item() ``` Mean over the 26 images to get per-head δ. Rank all 576 heads. Top-12 enters Figure 2. The L11.H14 cross-reference happens here too. The paper's §4.3 quotes rank 141/576 for that head — `np.argsort(delta_flat)` with reverse indexing gets you the number. For Figure 2's panel C (the 4-head × 3-image side-by-side attention maps), pick three representative images with structured output content (docvqa, receipt, form) and plot the OCR2 and base attention matrices side by side for the top-4 heads. Anchor the visual story that "head 14.7 consistently lights up on signatures" to a specific image ID in the manifest. Output: `code/results/attention_diff.json`, `code/figures/fig2_attention_diff.pdf`, plus the per-category marker-attention diagnostic (`fig2b_marker_attention.pdf`) that tells you which heads *gain* marker-attention under fine-tuning (L30+) and which *lose* it (L0). ## Stage 5 — Residual-stream activation patching (the causal test) ```bash python code/experiments/08b_residual_patching_lite.py ``` This is the FCCT-style three-pass procedure, simplified to one direction (OCR2 → base). Per test image, for each layer L in [0..35]: 1. **Cache run.** Forward OCR2 on image x, cache `h_L(x)` — OCR2's residual stream at layer L. 2. **Natural run.** Forward base on x, record last-position logits `π_natural`. 3. **Patched run.** Forward base on x again; at layer L+1 entry, replace the residual stream with the cached `h^{OCR2}_L(x)`; continue to layer 35; record last-position logits `π_patched(L)`. Two measurements per (image, layer): ```python kl = F.kl_div( F.log_softmax(pi_patched, dim=-1), F.softmax(pi_natural, dim=-1), reduction='sum', ) top1_flip = (pi_patched.argmax() == pi_ocr2.argmax()).item() ``` The paper tests on 4 representative images (docvqa, form, receipt, handwritten) out of the 26 — this is the biggest outstanding caveat in the paper, flagged explicitly in §4.2 and in the mock reviewer's comments. Same infrastructure would extend to all 26 at the cost of 37 × 22 = 814 more forward passes (~1–2 hours). Re-running on the full set is the first thing to do in a camera-ready pass. Output: `code/results/residual_patching.json`, `code/figures/fig4_causal_patching.pdf`. ## Stage 6 — Per-module weight diff ```bash python code/experiments/09b_weight_diff_streaming.py ``` Streaming version. The non-streaming precursor OOM'd trying to hold both models' weights in CPU memory. This one: 1. Loads the base model at bf16 on CPU, iterates through all 36 decoder layers extracting per-module state dicts (q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj, mlp.gate_proj, mlp.up_proj, mlp.down_proj), frees the model. 2. Loads OCR2 the same way. 3. For each (layer, module), computes `(W_ocr2 - W_base).norm(p='fro')` on CPU in bf16 float32-lifted. 4. Also runs a truncated SVD on each per-module diff tensor to compute rank-r recovery: ```python def rank_r_recovery(diff, r): _, S, _ = torch.linalg.svd(diff.float(), full_matrices=False) total = (S ** 2).sum() captured = (S[:r] ** 2).sum() return (captured / total).item() ``` This gives the rank-4 and rank-16 LoRA-feasibility numbers that back §4.5 of the paper. Output: `code/results/weight_diff_analysis.json`, `code/figures/fig5_weight_diff.pdf`. ## Figure rendering conventions All figures are rendered with matplotlib at dpi=180, `bbox_inches="tight"`, `facecolor="white"`. PDF (for LaTeX) and PNG (for the narrator companion) are both produced per figure. Captions are written in the human voice called for in the AI-fingerprint rules: specific, with layer numbers and head indices, without "illustrates the relationship between." The Figure 1 caption is the shortest useful example. Latex paper is compiled with `tectonic`: ```bash cd paper && tectonic main.tex ``` Not `pdflatex`, not `xelatex`, not `latexmk`. Tectonic is self-contained, auto-downloads missing packages, and doesn't require three bibliography passes. ## Compute budget, measured | Stage | Wall-clock on M4 Pro 24 GB | |---|---| | `01_build_eval_set.py` | 3–5 min (first run; datasets cache after) | | `02_extract_activations.py` × 3 | 30 min total (~10 min/model) | | `03_residual_diff.py` | 1 min | | `04_attention_diff.py` | 2 min | | `08b_residual_patching_lite.py` | 20 min (4 images × 36 layers) | | `09b_weight_diff_streaming.py` | 15 min (two model loads at bf16) | | Figures + LaTeX compile | 5 min | | **Total** | **~75 minutes** | Add ~15 minutes the first time for HuggingFace model downloads. Everything after the first run is cached on disk. ## Things to check if something breaks - **Empty attention tensors.** `attn_implementation` is not `"eager"`. Check model load. - **OOM during model load.** You have another model still in memory. Sequential-load pattern is load → forward → delete → empty_cache → load next. Don't skip the delete. - **Shape mismatch on activation diff.** `MIN_PIXELS != MAX_PIXELS`, or different between models. All three models must use the same fixed value; the script pins this. - **Shape mismatch on attention diff with GQA.** Qwen2.5-VL uses grouped-query attention with `num_key_value_heads=2`. The HF implementation broadcasts KV heads up to the query-head dim when returning `output_attentions`, so the returned attention tensor's second dim is 16 (query heads), not 2. If your tensor's second dim is 2, you're on a different HF version; update transformers. - **`09_weight_diff.py` OOM.** Use the streaming version (`09b_weight_diff_streaming.py`). Do not try to subtract two full model state dicts on a 24 GB machine. - **HuggingFace rate limit on Nanonets checkpoints.** `hf auth login` with a token. Anonymous pulls work most of the time but sometimes throttle under load. --- # Comprehension Questions *Ten questions across the major concepts in the paper. Each one tests understanding, not recall — if you can answer these without the paper open, you've internalized the work. Answers are in collapsible sections below each question.* --- ### 1. Why does the paper compute both a residual-stream L2 diff *and* a weight-space Frobenius diff? Aren't these measuring the same thing?
Answer They measure different things and disagree about where the fine-tune "acts." The residual-stream diff measures *effects* — how much the internal states of OCR2 and base diverge at each layer during a forward pass — and concentrates at the top of the decoder (peak at L35). The weight-space diff measures *cause* — which parameters actually changed — and is roughly uniform across all 36 layers (fractional change 0.06–0.09 per layer). Small parameter edits at early layers compound through the forward pass to produce outsized effects at the end. The paper runs both because either one alone gives an incomplete picture.
--- ### 2. The paper distinguishes between "L=11 is the causal threshold" and "L=35 is the magnitude peak." Why is this distinction load-bearing?
Answer They answer different questions. L=35 is where the *magnitude* of divergence peaks — the residual streams of OCR2 and base are most different at that layer. L=11 is the *lowest* layer at which patching OCR2's residual into the base flips 100% of tested images. After L11, the base model's layers 12–35 can no longer pull the distribution back to the base's natural answer. The fine-tune has "committed" causally by L11 even though the divergence continues to grow through L35. Conflating the two would lead to saying "the fine-tune is at L35," which misrepresents the causal story.
--- ### 3. Why does the paper refuse to call L0.H13 a "structured-output emission head" even though it's the top head in the attention-pattern diff?
Answer Because layer-0 attention happens before any autoregressive generation. At layer 0, the model is looking at freshly embedded image patches and the prompt tokens — there are no output tokens yet for a head to emit. The "emission" interpretation is mechanically impossible at that position. The marker-attention diagnostic (Figure 2b in the paper) confirms this: L0 heads *decrease* attention to structured-marker tokens, while L30+ heads *increase* it. So the L0 cluster is doing input-side re-encoding of the visual stream, not output formatting. The paper narrates these as two separate phenomena.
--- ### 4. Hua et al.'s Qwen2.5-VL taxonomy classifies L11.H14 as a router head and L19.H26, L13.H26 as promoter heads. The paper only cross-checks L11.H14. Why not the other two?
Answer Architecture mismatch. Hua et al. indexed their heads against Qwen2.5-VL-7B, which has 28 heads per layer. The paper analyzes Qwen2.5-VL-3B, which has 16 heads per layer. Head index H26 doesn't exist in a 16-head layer — it's not an addressable location in our model. L11.H14 is the only head whose index is valid in both 7B and 3B variants, so it's the only one we can cross-check. The paper states this limitation explicitly rather than glossing over it.
--- ### 5. Two independently-trained fine-tunes of the same base produce residual-diff curves that correlate at Pearson r = 0.996 across all 37 layers. What does this specifically tell us, and what does it *not* tell us?
Answer It tells us the layer-concentration pattern is a property of the base model's geometry, not of any particular fine-tune's training data. OCR-s and OCR2-3B were trained on related but not identical datasets; if they localize identically, the localization is driven by what the base model permits, not by what the training data demanded. This is the main corroboration for Claim 1. It does *not* tell us the two fine-tunes are doing the same thing functionally — they could have perfectly correlated L2-diff curves while computing different functions. Functional equivalence would need output-level or patching-level evidence, which the paper doesn't provide across the two fine-tunes.
--- ### 6. What's the difference between the "top-12 Frobenius heads" (Figure 2a) and the "top marker-attention gainers" (Figure 2b)? Why does the paper compute both?
Answer The Frobenius diff measures how much a head's *entire* attention matrix changed. The marker-attention diagnostic measures how much a head's attention specifically to structured-marker token positions changed. These detect different things — a head can have a large total attention shift (high Frobenius) without its attention to markers changing at all, and vice versa. The paper reports that zero of the top-12 Frobenius heads are also in the top-20 marker-attention gainers. The single cleanest marker-specialization head is L4.H13, which sits outside the Frobenius top-12. The two measurements are not redundant and the paper would be weaker if it only ran one.
--- ### 7. The paper reports that 69% of the weight-space Frobenius change lives in three MLP projections (gate, up, down). Why does this support a rank-16 LoRA prescription, and what's the gap between this observation and a strong LoRA claim?
Answer The SVD of each per-module weight-diff tensor shows that a rank-16 approximation recovers at least 60% of the Frobenius energy on the MLP projections. If most of the fine-tune's weight change sits in a low-rank subspace of those specific modules, then a LoRA on those modules at that rank should be able to *represent* the change — suggesting rank-16 LoRA on `mlp.gate_proj` and `mlp.up_proj` across all decoder layers is a reasonable starting point. The gap: SVD recovery of the Frobenius norm is not the same as a rank-16 LoRA *trained from scratch* actually matching OCR2 on DocVQA. You'd have to train the LoRA and measure. The paper frames this as a hypothesis, not a result; the mock reviewer pushed back and the paper acknowledges the overreach.
--- ### 8. Why doesn't the structured-output probe (Claim 3 in the spec) make it into the final paper as a figure?
Answer Two rounds of probe design, both unsatisfying. The original target — distinguishing structured-marker token positions from content-token positions — turned out to be position-trivial. The Nanonets prompt places the marker tokens at a fixed suffix position, so any classifier can predict the label from position alone, telling us nothing about representational content. The pivot to image-token vs. text-token modality-boundary probing produced a ceiling-effect null: both base and OCR2 achieve 100% at every layer, because modality decodability at layer 0 is linearly trivial in VLMs. The paper documents the pivot in the methods section, omits Figure 3, and lists a better-targeted probe (e.g., balanced "does this page contain a watermark" on pre-output residuals) as future work. Trying to rescue a ceiling-effect null would have been worse than reporting the honest null.
--- ### 9. The paper calls the activation-patching result a "sufficiency test." Why *sufficiency* rather than *necessity*, and what would it take to establish necessity?
Answer Sufficiency: we show that injecting OCR2's residual into the base at layer L is *enough* to flip the base's output to OCR2's. This tells us OCR2's state at L carries OCR-specific behavior. It does *not* tell us L is *required* — maybe OCR2 carries the same behavior at L-1 or L+1 as well, and the base's downstream layers would have picked it up either way. Establishing necessity would require an ablation in OCR2 — e.g., zero out the residual at L or mask specific heads at L and measure whether OCR2's output degrades. The paper doesn't run this (head-level patching is listed as future work in §5 and in the mock reviewer's questions). Causal methods in interp distinguish the two routinely, and getting the language right matters.
--- ### 10. What makes the L11.H14 rank-141 finding (router head "not substantially perturbed") a cleaner claim than the top-12 attention-diff finding?
Answer Three reasons. First, it's *a priori specified* — Hua et al. named L11.H14 specifically before our paper looked at it, so we're testing a pre-registered hypothesis, not hunting through 576 heads for a story. Second, it's *falsifiable and falsified in a clean direction*: if the fine-tune had rewired cross-modal arbitration, L11.H14 should be in our top-tier, and it isn't (24.5th percentile). Third, the *phrasing is honest*: "the attention pattern is not substantially perturbed" is what our method can show; "the router circuit is intact" would imply a functional claim our method cannot make (attention patterns can shift while function is preserved). The top-12 finding, by contrast, requires the reader to trust our post-hoc grouping of heads and our interpretation of what L0 vs. L30 clusters mean. The L11.H14 result needs no such trust — it's one head, one rank, one sentence. That's why the paper leads §4 with it rather than with the top-12 list.
--- ## Integration exercises If you want to stress-test your understanding beyond the per-question level: - **Sketch Figure 1 and Figure 4 on the same x-axis.** The residual diff peaks at L35; the causal patching KL peaks also rise through L35 (KL=8.07) but the top-1 flip rate saturates at L11. What does the shape of the two curves together tell you about how fine-tuning commits gradually vs. irreversibly? - **Predict what a hypothetical Figure 6 should look like** if the paper ran bidirectional patching (base → OCR2 in addition to OCR2 → base). Where would you expect the reverse-direction causal threshold to sit — L=11 as well? Earlier? Later? - **The MoE transfer argument.** The 3B findings don't translate directly to a 35B MoE flagship. But Tigges et al. showed circuit algorithms are stable across 70M–2.8B scale. What specific sub-claim in our paper would you be most confident transfers? Least confident? - **Design the next experiment.** Given the weight-diff result (69% in MLPs, concentrated in gate/up), what's the single cheapest follow-up experiment that would turn our rank-16 LoRA observation into a trained-LoRA result? What would you measure?
